# -*- sh -*-
# See http://rigaux.org/language-study/diagram.html for the format of this file
#claude: auto-generated by Claude Code (inspired from my other lineage.txt)

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Web browsers lineage
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

#src: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Timeline_of_web_browsers
#src: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Comparison_of_web_browsers
#src: wikipedia articles on different browsers

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Pre-Historical
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

*WorldWideWeb
  1990
  SY:2
  # Tim Berners-Lee, first browser ever, NeXT only, renamed Nexus
  # also an editor (read-write web)

Line Mode Browser
  1991
  WorldWideWeb
  # CERN, text-only, cross-platform, by Nicola Pellow

*webpage
  1991
  WorldWideWeb
  # Tom Duff, Bell Labs, Plan 9's first web browser

*ViolaWWW
  1992
  WorldWideWeb
  # Pei-Yuan Wei, UC Berkeley, first browser with stylesheets and applets

Erwise
  1992
  WorldWideWeb
  # Helsinki University, first graphical browser for X Window

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Historical
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

*Mosaic
  1993
  ViolaWWW
  SY:2
  # NCSA, Marc Andreessen, popularized the web
  # inline images, first browser for the masses

*Netscape Navigator
  1994
  Mosaic
  SY:6
  # Andreessen + Jim Clark, dominant browser mid-90s
Netscape Communicator
  1997
  Netscape Navigator
Netscape 6
  2000
  Netscape Navigator
  # based on Mozilla/Gecko rewrite

Spyglass Mosaic
  1994
  Mosaic
  SY:6
  # commercial license of NCSA Mosaic, basis for IE

*Internet Explorer
  1995
  Spyglass Mosaic
  SY:6
  # Microsoft, won the first browser war via Windows bundling
Internet Explorer 3
  1997
  # JScript, CSS
Internet Explorer 4
  1997
  # DHTML
Internet Explorer 5
  1999
  # XMLHttpRequest (AJAX origins)
Internet Explorer 6
  2001
  # dominant for years, web development nightmare

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Open source (Gecko / Mozilla)
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

*Mozilla
  1998
  Netscape Navigator
  SY:2
  # Netscape open-sourced, full Gecko rewrite from scratch

*Firefox
  2004
  Mozilla
  SY:2
  # originally Phoenix, then Firebird
  # broke IE's monopoly, popularized extensions
Firefox 3
  2008
Firefox 4
  2011
  # rapid release cycle started
Firefox Quantum
  2017
  # major rewrite, Servo components (Stylo CSS engine)

SeaMonkey
  2005
  Mozilla
  # continuation of Mozilla Suite (browser + mail + editor)

Camino
  2002
  Mozilla
  # native macOS Gecko browser, discontinued 2013

Tor Browser
  2008
  Firefox
  # Tor Project, Firefox fork hardened for anonymity over the Tor network

Pale Moon
  2009
  Firefox
  # long-running Firefox fork, kept old XUL/extension model;
  # later forked Gecko into its own engine, Goanna

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Open source (WebKit / Blink)
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

*KHTML
  1998
  Mosaic
  # KDE project, HTML rendering engine for Konqueror;
  # from-scratch implementation, spiritual descendant of Mosaic-era renderers

Konqueror
  2000
  KHTML
  # KDE browser/file manager

*WebKit
  2003
  KHTML
  SY:2
  # Apple forked KHTML for Safari

*Safari
  2003
  WebKit
  SY:6
  # Apple, default on macOS/iOS
Safari iOS
  2007

Epiphany
  2003
  WebKit
  # GNOME Web, switched from Gecko to WebKit in 2008

midori
  2007
  WebKit
  # lightweight WebKit/GTK browser; Raspberry Pi default for several years

*Chromium
  2008
  WebKit
  SY:2
  # Google open-source browser project
  # forked WebKit rendering into Blink in 2013

*Chrome
  2008
  Chromium
  SY:6
  # Google, V8 JavaScript engine
  # won the second browser war, now dominant
Chrome 4
  2010
  # extensions
Chrome 28
  2013
  # switched from WebKit to Blink

Blink
  2013
  WebKit
  # Google's fork of WebKit, powers Chromium and all its derivatives

*Edge (Chromium)
  2020
  Chromium
  SY:6
  # Microsoft rebuilt Edge on Chromium, replacing EdgeHTML

Opera (Blink)
  2013
  Chromium
  # Opera switched from Presto to Blink

Brave
  2016
  Chromium
  # Brendan Eich (JavaScript creator), privacy-focused

Vivaldi
  2016
  Chromium
  # by former Opera developers

Arc
  2022
  Chromium
  # The Browser Company, UI-focused

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Industry (historical engines)
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

*Opera (Presto)
  1995
  Mosaic
  SY:6
  # Norwegian, own Presto engine, innovative (tabs, mouse gestures)
Opera 7
  2003
  # Presto engine
Opera 12
  2012
  # last Presto version

Edge (EdgeHTML)
  2015
  Internet Explorer
  SY:6
  # Microsoft, new engine replacing Trident, abandoned for Chromium in 2020

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Text-mode
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

*Lynx
  1992
  WorldWideWeb
  SY:2
  # University of Kansas, text-only, still maintained

*w3m
  1995
  WorldWideWeb
  SY:2
  # Akinori Ito, Tohoku University; independent of Lynx
  # name from "WWW-wo-Miru" ("see the WWW" in Japanese)
  # text browser with tables and frames support

*Links
  1999
  Lynx
  SY:2
  # text browser, later gained graphical mode (Links2)

ELinks
  2001
  Links
  # enhanced Links fork

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Plan9
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

*mothra
  1995
  webpage
  SY:1
  # Plan 9 web browser, uses libpanel

*abaco
  2004
  mothra
  SY:1
  # Plan 9 web browser, by Federico Benavento; uses libhtml by Russ Cox

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Exotic / Minimal
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

Dillo
  1999
  # very lightweight, own rendering engine, FLTK-based

*NetSurf
  2003
  # own rendering engine, originally for RISC OS

surf
  2009
  WebKit
  # suckless project, minimal WebKit browser

qutebrowser
  2014
  Chromium
  # keyboard-driven, vim-like, Python + Qt

*Ladybird
  2022
  NetSurf
  # SerenityOS browser, own engine, used NetSurf libs early on

Lagrange
  2020
  # graphical client for the Gemini protocol;
  # not a web browser strictly, but a hypertext-document viewer in the same spirit

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Mobile (early)
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

Opera Mini
  2005
  Opera (Presto)
  SY:6
  # server-side rendering, huge in developing world

UC Browser
  2004
  SY:6
  # UCWeb, dominant in Asia

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Research / Influential
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

*Amaya
  1996
  WorldWideWeb
  # W3C reference browser/editor by Tim Berners-Lee, used to test web standards

*Servo
  2012
  Mozilla
  # Mozilla research, Rust-based parallel rendering engine
  # components merged into Firefox Quantum
Servo (LF)
  2023
  Servo
  # transferred to the Linux Foundation after Mozilla's 2020 layoffs;
  # active development resumed in 2024-2025 (parallel layout, WebGPU,
  # JavaScript via SpiderMonkey)
